content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 25, 2002

Michael R. Jones
City of Chico Park Commissioner
2411 Notre Dame Boulevard
Chico, CA 95928

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-256

Dear Mr. Jones:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question concerns your duties generally rather than any specific decision, we are treating your request as a request for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) Additionally, please note, nothing in this letter should be construed to apply to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) and (c)(4)(A).) Please also note that other conflict-of-interest laws outside the jurisdiction of the Act might apply to you. You may wish to consult with your city attorney concerning these other laws.

QUESTION

Does your volunteer activity create a conflict of interest under the Act?

CONCLUSION

Conflicts of interests under the Act are based on financial effects. So long as you do not make, participate in making, or influence a decision in which you have an economic interest, as set forth below, you will not have a conflict of interest.

_______________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. I-02-256
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a park commissioner for the City of Chico. Your powers and duties
include the operation and maintenance of Chico parks, by providing policy direction to
your city manager and his staff.

You have a large wildland park (Bidwell Park) for which you approve trail
projects. You have full authority to approve or veto the projects, and can allow particular
volunteers to adopt particular trails.

You are an active trail volunteer and have done considerable trail work for the
city prior to your recent appointment to the commission. You are a dentist, and have no
financial interest in any trail company. You have no income relating to any trail work,
trail guidebooks, etc. You only source of income is your dental business, interest on your
bank accounts and investment income (or loss) from diversified mutual funds.

The issue has been raised in the local newspaper whether there is a conflict of
interest. For example, you propose a trail project, you participate in the decision making
as a commissioner, and you volunteer to build it at your own expense. While doing the
work, you function as a private volunteer under the direction of your city staff.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the
prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides the
following eight-step analysis.

Step One: Are you a "public official?"

A public official is any "member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or
local government agency…." (Section 82048.) "Member" is defined to include "salaried
or unsalaried members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking
authority." (Reg. 18701(a).) Because you are a member of the City of Chico's Park and
Playground Commission, you are a public official under the Act. (See e.g., Barker
Advice Letter, No. I-01-133.)

Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Reg. 18702.1.) A public official "participates
in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and

File No. I-02-256
Page No. 3

without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision.
(Reg. 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears
before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Reg.
18702.3.)

Your question concerns whether you may vote on approval or disapproval of trail
projects. The deliberation and vote is considered making and participating in making a
governmental decision.

Step Three: Do you have an economic interest?

Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests. There are six possible economic interests:

1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a));

2. A public official has an economic interest in which he or she is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section
87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

3. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);

4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3);

5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(c); regulation 18703.4);

6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
known as the "personal financial effects" rule (section 87103, regulation 18703.5).

According to your facts, none of your economic interests are at issue in the
decisions in question. You are a dentist, and have no financial interest in any trail
company. You have no income relating to any trail work, trail guidebooks, etc. You note
that you are an active trail volunteer and have done considerable trail work for the city

File No. 1-02-256
Page No. 4

prior to your appointment. A conflict of interest under the Act can exist only if a decision
you will be making, participating in, or influencing will have a financial effect on your
economic interests. Since you have not described any decision that will have such an
effect, we cannot advise you further. We have enclosed a publication that describes the
remaining steps in the analysis.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

Enc: "Can I Vote? Conflict of Interest Overview"
Regulation 18329

economic interest, as set forth below, you will not have a conflict of interest.
